planting.note: Plant container-grown grafted trees in spring after all danger of frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; starfruit is frost-tender and should be grown in containers in zones 8–9 or planted in frost-protected microclimates.

directions: Plant container-grown, grafted trees in spring after frost risk has passed in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; purchase grafted trees for earlier bearing — seedling trees may take 5–7 years to fruit while grafted trees can bear in 1–2 years after planting. Averrhoa carambola is a tropical Southeast Asian fruit tree producing the unique, deeply 5-ridged, golden-yellow, translucent fruit known as starfruit or carambola that when sliced crosswise yields the distinctive five-pointed star shape; the fruit ranges from very tart to sweet depending on cultivar and is excellent fresh, in fruit salads, as a garnish, and in tropical beverages. Trees bloom and fruit multiple times per year in tropical climates, producing an almost continuous harvest in zones 10–11; in zone 9 they need frost protection and may lose leaves in cold winters but often recover. Sweet cultivars 'Arkin', 'Kari', and 'Fwang Tung' are the most popular for home orchards.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Tropical Research and Education Center

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or air-layered plants in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; bilimbi is more shade-tolerant than carambola and is a smaller, more compact tree excellent for home orchards in zone 10+.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings or air-layered trees in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; bilimbi is more shade-tolerant than its close relative starfruit and is a good choice for partially shaded spots in tropical gardens, though it fruits best in full sun. Averrhoa bilimbi is a relative of carambola producing clusters of small (2–4 inch), cucumber-shaped, intensely sour, pale-green to yellow fruits that grow directly from the trunk and main branches (cauliflory) in a bizarre but highly productive display; the extremely tart fruit is rarely eaten fresh but is valued in Southeast Asian and Caribbean cuisine for its acidity in curries, preserves, pickles, and beverages, substituting for tamarind or lime. Trees fruit heavily multiple times per year in frost-free climates (zones 10–12) and can be grown in large containers moved indoors in zone 9; the pinnate leaves and clustered trunk fruits make it an ornamental specimen even when not fruiting.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Tropical Research and Education Center
Purdue University Center for New Crops

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; peanut butter fruit is a compact shrub or small tree suited to container culture in zones 9 and below.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; peanut butter fruit is a compact, attractive tropical shrub-tree that grows well in containers and can be moved indoors in zone 9 to protect from frost. Bunchosia armeniaca is a South American tropical shrub or small tree in the malpighia family producing clusters of small, oval, orange to red fruits with soft, dry, sticky flesh that has an unmistakable flavor and aroma genuinely reminiscent of smooth peanut butter — making it one of the most novel and conversation-starting tropical fruits for home growers. The glossy, dark-green leaves and yellow flowers also make it a handsome ornamental shrub; it can fruit year-round in zones 10–12 and makes an excellent patio container specimen in cooler zones. Fruits are eaten fresh, used in smoothies, or spread like a condiment.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Tropical Research and Education Center
Purdue University Center for New Crops

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; African mangosteen is more cold-hardy than the tropical mangosteen (G. mangostana) and tolerates light frost once established.

directions: Plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; African mangosteen tolerates brief light frosts to about 28°F when established, making it considerably more cold-hardy than tropical mangosteen and viable in zone 9 sheltered locations. Garcinia livingstonei is an African native shrub or small tree grown for its small (1–2 inch), orange-yellow to orange-red, edible fruits with juicy, sweet-tart, apricot-like flesh and 1–3 large seeds; the flavor is pleasant and described as a combination of apricot and mango, and the fruits are eaten fresh, made into juices, and used to make a fermented beverage in southern Africa. Unlike tropical mangosteen (G. mangostana), which is notoriously difficult to cultivate outside the humid tropics, African mangosteen is adaptable, drought-tolerant when established, and significantly more cold-hardy — an excellent choice for Gulf Coast subtropical gardens in zones 9–11.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Tropical Research and Education Center
Purdue University Center for New Crops

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Padang cassia is the cinnamon most commonly sold in North American grocery stores and is more cold-hardy than true Ceylon cinnamon.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic soil (pH 4.5–6.0) in full sun; Padang cassia grows vigorously once established in frost-free conditions and begins producing harvestable bark on young shoots within 2–3 years after planting. Cinnamomum burmannii is an Indonesian native and the species that provides the majority of "cinnamon" sold in North American grocery stores and spice jars; it is harvested by stripping the bark from young branches and allowing it to dry into the familiar quills, and has a strong, sweet-spicy, slightly more pungent flavor and higher coumarin content than true Ceylon cinnamon (C. verum). The tree is an attractive, medium-sized, evergreen with oval, three-veined, cinnamon-scented leaves and can be grown as a landscape ornamental in zone 9–10; harvest bark from young shoots 1–2 years old and re-coppice to regenerate the plant cyclically for ongoing production.
sources: Purdue University Center for New Crops
